bodyHtml:
How Pharmaceutical Market Research Works in MENA: Methods, Data Sources, and What It Costs

Pharmaceutical market research in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) follows broadly similar principles to research in Western markets — but the data infrastructure, regulatory context, physician recruitment dynamics, and cultural considerations are substantially different. For pharmaceutical companies commissioning research in MENA for the first time, or evaluating research suppliers for GCC and Egypt studies, understanding how the research actually works is essential for making well-informed commissioning decisions.

This guide covers the primary research methods used in MENA pharmaceutical market research, how data is collected in each GCC market, what it costs, and how to evaluate the quality of research suppliers operating in the region.

The Four Core Pharmaceutical Market Research Methods in MENA

1. Quantitative Physician Surveys

The most frequently commissioned type of pharmaceutical market research. A structured questionnaire is designed around specific research objectives — brand awareness, prescribing intent, message recall, treatment pathway mapping, competitor positioning — and administered to a representative sample of physicians in the target specialty and geography.

How recruitment works in MENA

Physician recruitment in GCC markets requires verification against national physician registration databases. In Saudi Arabia, the Saudi Commission for Health Specialties (SCHS) is the primary registration body. In UAE, DHA (Dubai) and DOH (Abu Dhabi) maintain separate registers. In Kuwait, the Kuwait Medical Association. In Egypt, the Egyptian Medical Syndicate. Reputable research agencies maintain physician panels pre-verified against these databases, enabling rapid recruitment of qualified respondents.

Survey administration methods

CATI (Computer-Assisted Telephone Interview) is common for shorter surveys (15–25 minutes) and markets where physicians are accessible by phone. Online panel surveys are increasingly used across the GCC where smartphone penetration is high. Face-to-face structured interviews are used for longer questionnaires, complex topics, or markets where telephone/online accessibility is lower (parts of Egypt, for example). All three methods are available through BioNixus across all MENA markets.

Typical specifications

  • Sample size: 50–400 physicians per country per wave
  • Survey length: 15–45 minutes
  • Turnaround: 3–7 weeks from study design confirmation
  • Languages: Arabic (Gulf dialect or Egyptian dialect as appropriate) and English
  • Compliance: ICH-GCP — informed consent, data protection, anonymisation

What quantitative physician surveys tell you

Brand awareness and recall levels, prescribing frequency and patient share, treatment decision drivers, competitive positioning, message comprehension and persuasiveness, unmet medical needs, and physician perceptions of disease and treatment options.

What they do not tell you

Actual sales volumes, real-world patient outcomes, patient-level adherence data, or procurement and pricing dynamics. Physician-reported prescribing behaviour is a self-reported proxy for actual prescribing — it can differ from dispensing records due to recall bias and social desirability effects.

2. Hospital Sales Data Collection

The second core method — and one of BioNixus's primary differentiating capabilities in MENA. Hospital sales data is collected directly from the source of pharmaceutical dispensing: hospital pharmacy information systems (PIS), hospital procurement records, and physician prescription data.

How it works

BioNixus recruits a panel of hospitals in each GCC country and Egypt — typically 20–30% of the total hospital universe. Each panel hospital submits monthly dispensing data from its pharmacy information system. This data is cleaned, validated, and projected to the full hospital universe using stratification weights based on hospital size, type (public/private, teaching/non-teaching), and specialty profile.

Primary data sources

  • Pharmacy dispensing records — actual units dispensed from hospital pharmacy by molecule, SKU, and in some cases department
  • Procurement data — purchase orders submitted to NUPCO (Saudi Arabia), CMS (Kuwait), HMC (Qatar), or direct distributors — reflecting sell-in volumes
  • Physician prescribing diaries — monthly reports from standing physician panels recording indication-level and patient-level prescribing, layered onto pharmacy data to add indication and patient context

How this differs from IQVIA audit data

IQVIA's GCC pharmaceutical audit is based on panel projections from a sample of pharmacies and distributors, without patient-level or indication-level resolution. BioNixus collects primary-source data with hospital-level, department-level, indication-level, and patient-level granularity — providing deeper insight than standard audit products, particularly in markets where IQVIA's panel coverage is thin.

3. KOL Mapping and Medical Affairs Research

Key Opinion Leader (KOL) mapping is the research process of identifying, profiling, and ranking the physicians most influential in shaping clinical practice in a given therapeutic area and geography.

How KOL mapping works in MENA

BioNixus combines three data sources for MENA KOL identification:

Publication analysis — systematic review of scientific publications authored by MENA-based physicians in target therapeutic areas, using bibliometric databases (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science). Publication count, citation index, journal impact factor, and co-authorship networks are analysed.

Congress and advisory board activity — BioNixus tracks GCC and MENA physician participation in international and regional congresses (ASCO, ASH, ESC, ADA, and regional equivalents) as speakers, moderators, poster presenters, and guideline committee members.

Prescribing reach and institutional affiliation — A KOL's clinical influence is proportional to the number of patients under their care and the seniority of their institutional role. BioNixus assesses each identified KOL's departmental seniority, patient throughput, and ability to influence peer prescribing.

Output: Tiered KOL list (Tier 1 national/international leaders, Tier 2 regional/institutional leaders, Tier 3 emerging voices), influence heat maps by institution and geography, and recommended engagement strategy by tier.

4. Market Access and Payer Research

Market access research maps the pathway from product registration to patient access — including regulatory approval, formulary inclusion, reimbursement, and any additional steps required for a pharmaceutical product to reach patients in each GCC market.

What market access research covers in MENA

  • SFDA registration pathway and timeline (Saudi Arabia)
  • NUPCO tender strategy, historical award data, and pricing benchmarks
  • MOH, NGHA, and private sector formulary processes and timelines
  • DHA, DOH, and MOHAP formulary inclusion requirements (UAE)
  • CMS Kuwait and HMC Qatar tender and formulary processes
  • Payer decision-maker identification and interview programmes
  • HTA (Health Technology Assessment) evidence requirements by country
  • Pricing benchmarking across GCC reference markets

How to Evaluate Pharmaceutical Market Research Suppliers in MENA

When commissioning pharmaceutical market research in MENA, these are the quality indicators that distinguish strong suppliers:

Physician panel verification: Ask how physicians are recruited and how their credentials are verified. Reputable agencies verify against national registration databases (SCHS, DHA, DOH, MOHP). Agencies that use unverified online panels cannot guarantee respondent quality.

Direct vs. subcontracted fieldwork: Ask whether fieldwork is conducted by the agency's own team or subcontracted. Subcontracted fieldwork introduces quality variability and reduces visibility into data collection processes.

Arabic-language capability: Ask to see sample questionnaires in Arabic. Native-language questionnaire development is different from mechanical translation — poorly translated instruments affect response quality.

Hospital data methodology: Ask specifically what data sources are used, how hospitals are recruited, what the panel size is, and what projection methodology is applied.

ICH-GCP compliance: All pharmaceutical market research involving healthcare professionals should be conducted under ICH-GCP protocols — informed consent, data protection, anonymisation, and ethics committee approval where required.

Typical Costs for Pharmaceutical Market Research in MENA

Research TypeTypical Cost RangeNotes
Quantitative physician survey — 1 country, 75–100 HCPsUSD 18,000–35,000Online panel or CATI
Quantitative physician survey — 1 country, 150–200 HCPsUSD 30,000–55,000
Multi-country GCC survey — 4 countries, 75 HCPs eachUSD 75,000–130,000Single vendor
KOL mapping — 1 country, 1 therapy areaUSD 15,000–30,000Publication + interview based
Qualitative IDIs — 10–15 KOL interviewsUSD 20,000–40,000
Hospital sales data — 1 country, monthlyUSD 25,000–60,000/yearSubscription
Market access research — 1 countryUSD 25,000–45,000
Full market entry study — 1 countryUSD 60,000–120,000Quant + qual + market access

These ranges reflect direct specialist agency costs. Global consulting firms applying overhead and margin typically charge 40–80% above these ranges for equivalent research.

How to Evaluate Pharmaceutical Market Research Suppliers in MENA

Key quality indicators: physician panel verification against national registration databases; direct vs. subcontracted fieldwork; native Arabic-language questionnaire capability; transparent hospital data methodology; and ICH-GCP compliance confirmation in writing.
Typical Costs for Pharmaceutical Market Research in MENA

faq.question: What is quantitative pharmaceutical market research?
faq.answer: Quantitative pharmaceutical market research involves structured surveys with statistically representative samples of physicians, patients, or other stakeholders — producing numerical data that can be analysed for frequency, percentage breakdowns, and statistical significance. In MENA, quantitative physician surveys are the most common form, typically involving 50–300 physicians per country per study wave, administered by CATI, online panel, or face-to-face interview.

faq.question: How are physicians recruited for pharmaceutical surveys in GCC markets?
faq.answer: In GCC markets, physicians are recruited against verified national registration databases — SCHS in Saudi Arabia, DHA and DOH in UAE, Kuwait Medical Association in Kuwait. Reputable agencies maintain standing physician panels pre-verified against these databases. BioNixus maintains proprietary verified physician panels across all GCC markets and Egypt.

faq.question: What data sources are used for pharmaceutical sales tracking in MENA?
faq.answer: BioNixus collects pharmaceutical sales data in MENA from three primary sources: hospital pharmacy dispensing records (collected directly from hospital PIS systems), procurement tender data (including publicly available NUPCO data in Saudi Arabia and CMS data in Kuwait), and physician prescribing diary panels for indication-level and patient-level data. This primary-source approach provides more granular and traceable data than standard panel-projection audit products.

faq.question: How long does a pharmaceutical market research project take in MENA?
faq.answer: A typical quantitative physician survey in one GCC country takes 3–7 weeks from study design finalisation to final report. Multi-country studies add 1–2 weeks for coordination. Qualitative projects (KOL interviews) typically take 4–8 weeks. Hospital sales data projects have a set-up phase of 4–8 weeks followed by monthly or quarterly data delivery.

faq.question: Which companies provide pharmaceutical market research in Dubai and GCC?
faq.answer: BioNixus is a specialist pharmaceutical and healthcare market research agency with 12+ years of direct GCC experience, offering physician surveys, hospital sales data, KOL mapping, and market access research across all GCC markets and Egypt. BioNixus operates from offices in London (UK), Sheridan Wyoming (USA), and Cairo (Egypt). Contact: admin@bionixus.com | +44 7727 666682.
